summary.category: Historical Time Measurement and Simple Pendulum
points: Humans measured time using natural cycles (Sun's rising/setting). Ancient devices included sundials, water clocks, and hourglasses. The simple pendulum, invented by Christiaan Huygens (inspired by Galileo), revolutionized timekeeping. A simple pendulum's time period (time for one oscillation) depends on its length but not on the bob's mass, making it reliable for measuring equal intervals of time.

Speed is the distance covered in unit time. The SI unit of speed is metre per second (m/s) or kilometre per hour (km/h). The fundamental relationship is: Speed = Distance / Time; Distance = Speed × Time; Time = Distance / Speed. Average speed is total distance divided by total time, used when speed varies during motion.

Linear motion is movement along a straight line. Uniform linear motion has constant speed and covers equal distances in equal time intervals. Non-uniform linear motion has changing speed and covers unequal distances in equal time intervals. Most real-world motion is non-uniform, making average speed concept essential for practical applications.
